text: When working on something that genuinely interests you, you can keep your attention on it for a long stretch without your mind wandering.
Background noise, people moving, or small interruptions usually derail your concentration completely.
Within a few minutes of starting a focused task, you often catch yourself doing something else without planning to.
You are comfortable spotting rules, regularities, or hidden structure behind concrete examples.
Abstract diagrams or purely symbolic explanations rarely feel clear unless tied to a concrete example.
You prefer instructions that list exact steps over explanations that stress the “big idea” behind them.
Bright lights, strong smells, or crowded noisy spaces can quickly feel overwhelming.
You hardly notice typical everyday sensory stimulation in busy shared spaces; it rarely bothers you.
Loud overlapping conversations feel like neutral background; they rarely drain your energy.
After several hours in lively group settings, you usually need quiet time alone to recharge.
Being around many people for a long time tends to energise you rather than wear you down.
You look forward to back-to-back social events on the same day and rarely feel “peopled out”.
Knowing the plan in advance and having predictable routines helps you feel settled and effective.
You are comfortable improvising when schedules change at the last minute and rarely find that stressful.
You prefer open-ended days with few fixed commitments over days blocked into timed slots.
When new information contradicts what you believed, you can revise your view without much inner resistance.
Once you have decided on an answer, you find it uncomfortable to revisit it even if someone raises a good counterpoint.
Ambiguous situations where there is no single “right” interpretation feel irritating rather than interesting.
You usually have a clear sense of how long tasks will take and rarely lose track of time when deadlines matter.
Hours can pass in what feels like minutes when you are absorbed; you often underestimate how much time went by.
You frequently run late or need alarms because internal time sense is weak compared to what the clock shows.
You notice early when you are hungry, thirsty, tired, or physically tense before it becomes severe.
You are often surprised by sudden hunger, fatigue, or tension because earlier signals were easy to miss.
Physical cues from your body feel vague or “far away” compared to what is happening in your surroundings.
One idea often sparks many side connections, analogies, or tangents you did not plan in advance.
Your thinking usually stays close to the task at hand; wide associative hops feel rare or unhelpful.
You prefer sticking to a linear outline rather than following interesting but off-topic mental branches.
Diagrams, maps, or mental imagery often help you understand faster than long verbal explanations alone.
You rely mainly on spoken or written language; pictures feel optional rather than central to how you think.
When learning something new, you prefer a clear verbal sequence over spatial or pictorial layouts.
You can return to a complex task after an interruption and pick up roughly where you left off.
Switching tasks frequently feels more natural than blocking long uninterrupted stretches.
You enjoy puzzles or games that reward noticing subtle patterns others overlook.
You rarely look for a deeper rule once a procedure “works well enough”.
Layered textures, perfumes, or fluorescent flicker can linger in your awareness long after exposure.
You can tune out sensory clutter for hours without feeling drained.
One-to-one conversations feel easier to sustain than free-flowing group banter.
You seek out large gatherings when you need a mood lift rather than when you need rest.
Checklists and written timelines reduce your anxiety more than verbal reminders alone.
Strict agendas feel suffocating even when they would make coordination easier.
You can hold two plausible interpretations of the same situation without forcing an immediate choice.
Changing a habit feels almost as hard as solving a technical problem from scratch.
You segment your day mentally into “before / after” anchor events rather than watching the clock constantly.
You rarely think about how long something took until someone asks you afterwards.
Noticing your breathing or heartbeat helps you gauge stress before it peaks.
You discover you are exhausted mainly from behaviour changes (irritability) rather than early body cues.
Metaphors and analogies come to mind automatically when you explain ideas to others.
You edit your speech to remove “too many” side threads before you say them out loud.
You can rotate or rearrange objects in your mind when judging whether they would fit in a space.
Written lists are your first tool; sketching or spatial models feel like an extra step.
When something important is coming up, I can picture how the next few weeks might unfold.

stemVariants.global: When something important is coming up, I can picture how the next few weeks might unfold.
stemVariants.ghana: When something big is coming up — a naming ceremony, exams, or travel — I can picture how the next few weeks might unfold.
stemVariants.west_africa: When something big is coming up — a naming ceremony, exams, or travel — I can picture how the next few weeks might unfold.

text: I start preparing for deadlines while they still feel far away on the calendar.

stemVariants.global: I start preparing for deadlines while they still feel far away on the calendar.
stemVariants.ghana: I start preparing while the date still feels far away on the calendar or in family talk.
stemVariants.west_africa: I start preparing while the date still feels far away on the calendar or in family talk.

text: Hours feel short when I am absorbed; I often look at the clock and realise more time passed than it felt like.
I bump tasks forward until the last stretch of pressure finally gets me moving.

stemVariants.global: I bump tasks forward until the last stretch of pressure finally gets me moving.
stemVariants.ghana: I bump tasks forward until the last stretch before market day, exams, or travel finally gets me moving.
stemVariants.west_africa: I bump tasks forward until the last stretch before market day, exams, or travel finally gets me moving.

text: I keep a rough map in my head of what belongs to which day this week.
I split big projects into ordered steps with rough time boxes before I begin.
I work in bursts; each stretch feels shorter or longer on the clock than it felt from the inside.
The order of small errands in a day feels flexible; sequence matters little to me.

stemVariants.global: The order of small errands in a day feels flexible; sequence matters little to me.
stemVariants.ghana: The order of small errands before market or after school feels flexible; sequence matters little to me.
stemVariants.west_africa: The order of small errands before market or after school feels flexible; sequence matters little to me.

text: I notice seasonal rhythms like holidays or exam cycles shaping how I plan ahead.

stemVariants.global: I notice seasonal rhythms like holidays or exam cycles shaping how I plan ahead.
stemVariants.ghana: I notice seasons, holidays, harvest talk, or exam cycles shaping how I plan ahead.
stemVariants.west_africa: I notice seasons, holidays, harvest talk, or exam cycles shaping how I plan ahead.

text: Long stretches of free time feel shapeless until I give them a simple frame.
I replay past conversations on a loose timeline to learn what to do differently next time.
I arrive close to start times and leave little spare time for traffic or queues.

stemVariants.global: I arrive close to start times and leave little spare time for traffic or queues.
stemVariants.ghana: I arrive close to start times and leave little spare time for traffic, tro-tro waits, or queues.
stemVariants.west_africa: I arrive close to start times and leave little spare time for traffic, tro-tro waits, or queues.

text: I can describe how my typical weekday flows from morning to evening.
I treat today and next week as about equally “present” when choosing what to worry about.
I picture where milestones sit on a calendar before saying yes to new commitments.
I lose track of clock time during screen time, reading, or deep hobbies.
I keep mental notes about what should happen before lunch versus after school or work.
I feel steady when there is a clear rhythm to the week even if plans change inside it.
I underestimate how long familiar tasks take almost every time.
I enjoy imagining possible futures in detail when making a big choice.
When a choice feels heavy, I sense tightness, warmth, or openness in my body before the words line up.

stemVariants.global: When a choice feels heavy, I sense tightness, warmth, or openness in my body before the words line up.
stemVariants.ghana: When a choice feels heavy — at the market or in a family talk — I sense tightness, warmth, or openness in my body before the words line up.
stemVariants.west_africa: When a choice feels heavy — at the market or in a family talk — I sense tightness, warmth, or openness in my body before the words line up.

text: I pick up early signs of hunger, thirst, or tiredness while they are still mild.
Other people seem to notice hunger or fatigue before I feel it clearly.
Signals from my body feel faint compared to what is happening around me.
I sense heart rate or breathing shift during excitement or stress while it is happening.
Muscle tension in shoulders, jaw, or hands is something I notice during a busy day.
I realise I need water or food only after a headache or irritability shows up.
Fullness after eating reaches my awareness quickly enough to guide portion pace.
I track small shifts in energy across the day like a weather map inside me.
Physical discomfort sits in the background until it suddenly demands attention.
I sense temperature comfort in a room soon after entering.
Butterflies or calm in the stomach show up during anticipation or relief.
I connect shallow breathing with stress early enough to slow down on purpose.
I am last to notice that the room feels too loud or too bright for my comfort.
I name inner states with simple words like wired, flat, or buzzing.
I feel sleep pressure build gradually before a sharp drop in energy hits.
I notice posture slipping into a slouch before discomfort spikes.
I sense rest needs during social time before irritability leaks into my tone.
I rely on outside cues like meal times to guess my hunger more than inner cues.

stemVariants.global: I rely on outside cues like meal times to guess my hunger more than inner cues.
stemVariants.ghana: I rely on meal times or what others are eating to guess my hunger more than inner cues.
stemVariants.west_africa: I rely on meal times or what others are eating to guess my hunger more than inner cues.

text: A short body scan in my mind helps me locate where stress sits.
While working on one thing, my mind links useful ideas from topics that seemed unrelated.

stemVariants.global: While working on one thing, my mind links useful ideas from topics that seemed unrelated.
stemVariants.ghana: While doing one task — selling, studying, or fixing something — my mind links useful ideas from topics that seemed unrelated.
stemVariants.west_africa: While doing one task — selling, studying, or fixing something — my mind links useful ideas from topics that seemed unrelated.

text: Analogies between different areas of life show up when I let my mind wander freely.
My thinking stays close to the task; wide hops feel rare or distracting.
I prefer a straight outline and skip tempting side branches.
A single word can pull up a chain of memories from different years.
I spot parallels between a story someone tells and something I read elsewhere.
Brainstorming lists grow wide fast; pruning them down is the harder part.
I keep meetings and lessons on one track; side topics seldom pull me off course.
Dreams or daydreams reuse symbols that connect to daytime worries.
I draw creative links between tools at hand when a usual approach is blocked.
I lose the main thread when a conversation jumps topics quickly.
I enjoy mixing metaphors from cooking, music, and travel to explain an idea.
I solve puzzles fastest when I stop forcing a linear path and let patterns pop.
I screen out interesting tangents so the group finishes on time.
Remote connections feel obvious in hindsight even if they surprised me at first.
I map how two school subjects support each other across the term.
I often find humour in the collision between two unlike frames.
I stick to one interpretation until evidence forces a switch.
I use mind maps or loose webs more often than tight bullet lists when planning ideas.
I notice when a new idea rhymes with an older habit I thought I left behind.
Diagrams, sketches, or maps speed up my understanding more than words alone.

stemVariants.global: Diagrams, sketches, or maps speed up my understanding more than words alone.
stemVariants.ghana: A quick sketch in sand or on paper, or pointing on a map, speeds up my understanding more than words alone.
stemVariants.west_africa: A quick sketch in sand or on paper, or pointing on a map, speeds up my understanding more than words alone.

text: I rehearse sentences in my head until the wording feels right.
I picture layouts of rooms or streets to remember where things sit.
Narrating events in order with words is easier than drawing them.
I rotate shapes in imagination to see if parts will fit.
I take voice notes because speaking captures my thoughts cleaner than imagery.
I remember faces and scenes like snapshots more than spoken lines.
Written step lists carry me through a recipe or repair more than pictures.
I turn abstract rules into little verbal formulas I repeat.
I navigate by landmarks and sense of direction more than street names.

stemVariants.global: I navigate by landmarks and sense of direction more than street names.
stemVariants.ghana: I navigate by landmarks, paths people use, and sense of direction more than street signs alone.
stemVariants.west_africa: I navigate by landmarks, paths people use, and sense of direction more than street signs alone.

text: I explain new ideas to someone else using a chalk talk or whiteboard sketch.
I replay dialogue lines from films or books to savour rhythm and word choice.
I score higher on remembering patterns than on remembering exact phrases.
I translate teacher instructions into a quick inner comic strip.
Colour coding or spatial grouping organises my notes.
I trust a spoken promise more if I hear the exact words again in memory.
I imagine cutting objects or folding paper to guess outcomes.
I describe directions to a friend mainly as left-right verbal sequence.
Music feels like moving shapes or colours as much as sound layers.
I pick fonts and spacing when a message matters because form carries meaning.
